title: Summer garden simmer pot
subtitle: A light, green simmerpot of fresh mint, lemon verbena, cucumber, rosemary, and vanilla
excerpt: The summer counterpart to an autumn spice simmer pot: where autumn uses dried, dense aromatics, summer uses fresh herbs that release a clean, green, garden scent. The scent is lighter and more delicate, which suits a warm day with windows open.
sourceNotes: Fresh herb simmer pot method from natural homemaking references.

recipe.yieldDescription: One pot of summer fragrance (lasts 1 to 2 hours before needing top-up)
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Use fresh herbs on the day.
recipe.temperatureNote: Simmer at the lowest possible heat setting.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Use fresh each session. The simmered liquid can be refrigerated and used once more the next day. Discard after 48 hours.

glossaryTerms.definition: A saucepan of water with aromatic ingredients simmered on the lowest heat to release fragrance as steam. Fresh herbs release a brighter, greener scent than dried ones.

Fragrant molecules released at low temperatures that enter the air as vapour. Fresh mint, lemon verbena, and cucumber are high in light, bright volatile compounds that create the characteristic garden-fresh scent of this simmer pot.

Aloysia citrodora, a shrub with strongly lemon-scented leaves. It releases a clean, bright lemon fragrance when simmered, distinctly different from lemon peel or lemon balm. Lemon balm is an acceptable substitute.

content.text: evaporate cleanly at very low heat.

is the brightest contributor; lemon balm substitutes well if verbena is unavailable. Cucumber adds a cool, watery note. The vanilla pod grounds the blend with a faint warmth that prevents the scent from becoming too sharp. Fresh herbs last only one session at best; discard and start fresh for each use. Never leave the pot unattended on the hob.

items.prepNote: A spent pod from previous baking is fine

content.text: Add all ingredients to the saucepan with 1 litre of water. Set over the lowest heat and allow to come to the gentlest simmer. Top up with water every 30 minutes. Fresh herbs release their fragrance within the first 15 minutes; the scent is lighter than the autumn simmer pot and best enjoyed with the kitchen window open to let the steam circulate.

Use fresh ingredients each session. The cooled liquid can be strained and refrigerated to use once more within 48 hours. The herbs will be spent after the second use. Never leave the pot unattended on the hob.
